exercises.title: ConfigMap vs Secret — what's the difference?
exercises.instructions:
Which should hold each value?
A: DB URL (jdbc:postgresql://prod-postgres:5432/payments)
B: DB password
C: Log level (INFO)
D: Visa API key
E: Spring profile name (production)
exercises.expected_solution:
A: DB URL → ConfigMap (non-sensitive, env-specific, but not a secret)
B: DB password → Secret (sensitive credential)
C: Log level → ConfigMap (non-sensitive, env-specific)
D: Visa API key → Secret (sensitive API credential)
E: Spring profile name → ConfigMap or Deployment env var (non-sensitive)

test_cases.expected:
A: DB URL → ConfigMap (non-sensitive, env-specific, but not a secret)
B: DB password → Secret (sensitive credential)
C: Log level → ConfigMap (non-sensitive, env-specific)
D: Visa API key → Secret (sensitive API credential)
E: Spring profile name → ConfigMap or Deployment env var (non-sensitive)

hints: ConfigMap: anything you'd put in a properties file (not passwords)
Secret: anything that would be a security incident if leaked

exercises.title: Complete the Secret env var reference
exercises.instructions: Fill in blanks to inject Secret value as env var.
exercises.starter_code:
env:
- name: SPRING_DATASOURCE_PASSWORD
  valueFrom:
    ___KeyRef:
      name: payment-___
      key: db_password
exercises.expected_solution:
env:
- name: SPRING_DATASOURCE_PASSWORD
  valueFrom:
    secretKeyRef:
      name: payment-secrets
      key: db_password

blanks.hint: secretKeyRef for secrets; configMapKeyRef for ConfigMaps

hints: secretKeyRef vs configMapKeyRef: different source types
name: the k8s resource name; key: the key within that resource

exercises.title: Create a ConfigMap for Spring Boot
exercises.instructions:
Write ConfigMap with application.yml for Razorpay payment service prod:
1. DB URL, log level INFO, Zipkin URL
2. Tracing sampling: 0.1
3. Hikari pool size: 20
exercises.expected_solution:
# k8s/payment-configmap.yml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: payment-config
  namespace: payments
data:
  application.yml: |
    spring:
      datasource:
        url: jdbc:postgresql://prod-postgres:5432/payments
        hikari:
          maximum-pool-size: 20
          minimum-idle: 5
      zipkin:
        base-url: http://zipkin.infra:9411
    management:
      tracing:
        sampling:
          probability: '0.1'
    logging:
      level:
        com.razorpay: INFO
        org.springframework: WARN

# Mount in deployment:
# volumeMounts:
# - name: config
#   mountPath: /app/config
# volumes:
# - name: config
#   configMap:
#     name: payment-config
hints: Pipe (|) in YAML: multiline literal string for the application.yml content
Spring Boot auto-reads /app/config/application.yml if it exists
ConfigMap key = filename; value = file contents

exercises.title: Create Kubernetes Secret for DB credentials
exercises.instructions:
Write a Kubernetes Secret for Razorpay:
1. db_password: payments123 (base64 encode)
2. redis_password: redis123
3. visa_api_key: rzp_live_abc123
4. Show: how to create from command line (imperative) and how to apply YAML
exercises.expected_solution:
# Option A: Imperative (don't commit to git!):
# kubectl create secret generic payment-secrets \
#   --from-literal=db_password=payments123 \
#   --from-literal=redis_password=redis123 \
#   --from-literal=visa_api_key=rzp_live_abc123 \
#   --namespace payments

# Option B: YAML (base64 encode: echo -n 'value' | base64):
# echo -n 'payments123' | base64   → cGF5bWVudHMxMjM=
# echo -n 'redis123' | base64      → cmVkaXMxMjM=
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: payment-secrets
  namespace: payments
type: Opaque
data:
  db_password:  cGF5bWVudHMxMjM=   # base64(payments123)
  redis_password: cmVkaXMxMjM=     # base64(redis123)
  visa_api_key: cnpwX2xpdmVfYWJjMTIz  # base64(rzp_live_abc123)

# NEVER COMMIT THIS YAML WITH REAL VALUES TO GIT!
# Use: kubectl create (imperative) or external secret management (Vault)
hints: echo -n: no newline (important for base64 — newline changes the value)
type: Opaque: generic secret
NEVER commit Secret YAML with real values to Git

exercises.title: Deployment with ConfigMap volume + Secret env vars
exercises.instructions:
Write a Deployment that:
1. Mounts payment-config ConfigMap as /app/config volume
2. Injects db_password from payment-secrets as env var SPRING_DATASOURCE_PASSWORD
3. Injects visa_api_key as env var VISA_API_KEY
4. Sets SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=production as literal env var
exercises.expected_solution:
# Container spec (within Deployment):
containers:
- name: payment-service
  image: razorpay/payment-service:v2.1
  env:
  # Literal env var:
  - name: SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE
    value: production
  # From ConfigMap:
  - name: SPRING_CONFIG_ADDITIONAL_LOCATION
    value: /app/config/
  # From Secret:
  - name: SPRING_DATASOURCE_PASSWORD
    valueFrom:
      secretKeyRef:
        name: payment-secrets
        key: db_password
  - name: VISA_API_KEY
    valueFrom:
      secretKeyRef:
        name: payment-secrets
        key: visa_api_key
  volumeMounts:
  - name: config-volume
    mountPath: /app/config      # Spring Boot looks here
    readOnly: true
volumes:
- name: config-volume
  configMap:
    name: payment-config        # reads from ConfigMap
hints: readOnly: true: mounted config should never be modified by the app
SPRING_CONFIG_ADDITIONAL_LOCATION: tells Spring Boot to also check /app/config/
Both volume mount and env from secret can be used together

exercises.title: Graceful shutdown configuration
exercises.instructions:
Configure Spring Boot + k8s for graceful shutdown:
1. application.yml: server.shutdown=graceful
2. Deployment: preStop sleep 5s + terminationGracePeriodSeconds=60
3. @SpringBootTest: verify app responds to /actuator/health/readiness
4. Explain: what happens during pod termination with this config
hints: server.shutdown=graceful: allows in-flight requests to complete
preStop sleep: fills the gap between SIGTERM and iptables update
60s terminationGracePeriod > 5s preStop + 50s graceful shutdown: always has time

exercises.title: Spring Boot reading from ConfigMap and Secret
exercises.instructions:
Write @SpringBootTest verifying Spring Boot reads env vars:
1. Set DB_URL, DB_PASSWORD as env vars in test
2. @Value injection reads them correctly
3. Show: how ConfigMap values would be available via env or volume
hints: @TestPropertySource: override properties for specific tests
Spring Boot env binding: ALL_CAPS_UNDERSCORED maps to dotted.lower.case
Default values via ${prop:default}: safe for testing without real config

exercises.title: Kustomize for environment-specific deployments
exercises.instructions:
Write kustomize overlay structure for dev/staging/prod:
1. base/ with common deployment.yml
2. overlays/prod/ with production-specific patches
3. Patch: change replicas from 1 to 3 in prod
4. Show: kubectl apply with kustomize
exercises.expected_solution:
# k8s/base/deployment.yml (common):
# apiVersion: apps/v1
# kind: Deployment
# metadata: { name: payment-service }
# spec:
#   replicas: 1    ← dev default
#   template:
#     spec:
#       containers:
#       - name: payment-service
#         image: razorpay/payment-service:latest

# k8s/base/kustomization.yml:
# apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
# kind: Kustomization
# resources:
#   - deployment.yml
#   - service.yml

# k8s/overlays/prod/kustomization.yml:
# apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
# kind: Kustomization
# bases: [ ../../base ]
# images:
# - name: razorpay/payment-service
#   newTag: v2.1          ← specific version for prod
# patches:
# - target:
#     kind: Deployment
#     name: payment-service
#   patch: |
#     - op: replace
#       path: /spec/replicas
#       value: 3           ← 3 replicas in prod

# APPLY:
# kubectl apply -k k8s/overlays/prod/
# Or: kubectl diff -k k8s/overlays/prod/  (preview changes)

# BENEFITS:
# One base config, environment-specific overrides
# No duplication of 90% identical YAML
# Image tag pinned per environment (prod=v2.1, dev=latest)
# ConfigMap and Secret generation built-in to kustomize

public class KustomizeDemo { public static void main(String[] a) {
    System.out.println("kustomize: DRY environment-specific k8s configs"); } }
hints: bases: reference to common config
patches: JSON6902 patch operations on base resources
images: override image tags without editing deployment.yml

exercises.title: External Secrets Operator for Vault integration
exercises.instructions:
Design the External Secrets Operator (ESO) setup:
1. ExternalSecret CRD pointing to HashiCorp Vault
2. Creates k8s Secret from Vault path
3. Auto-refresh when Vault secret rotates
4. Explain: why ESO is better than manual Secret YAML in Git
exercises.expected_solution:
# ExternalSecret CRD (with External Secrets Operator installed):
apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: ExternalSecret
metadata:
  name: payment-external-secrets
  namespace: payments
spec:
  refreshInterval: 1h       # re-read from Vault hourly
  secretStoreRef:
    name: vault-backend
    kind: ClusterSecretStore
  target:
    name: payment-secrets    # creates/updates this k8s Secret
    creationPolicy: Owner
  data:
  - secretKey: db_password
    remoteRef:
      key: secret/payments/prod
      property: db_password
  - secretKey: visa_api_key
    remoteRef:
      key: secret/payments/prod
      property: visa_api_key

# ClusterSecretStore (Vault connection config):
apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: ClusterSecretStore
metadata:
  name: vault-backend
spec:
  provider:
    vault:
      server: 'https://vault.razorpay.internal'
      path: 'secret'
      version: 'v2'
      auth:
        kubernetes:
          mountPath: 'kubernetes'
          role: 'payment-service'

# WHY ESO BEATS MANUAL SECRET YAML:
# Problem: Secret YAML in Git → base64 values visible to anyone with repo access
# ESO solution:
#   Vault: encrypted, audited, RBAC-controlled secret store
#   ESO: reads from Vault → creates k8s Secret
#   Git: only contains ExternalSecret (which source, not the value)
#   Auto-rotate: when Vault secret changes → ESO updates k8s Secret → rolling restart
#   Audit: Vault logs every secret access (who, when, from which pod)

public class ESODemo { public static void main(String[] a) {
    System.out.println("ESO: Vault → k8s Secret automatic sync"); } }

expected_stdout_contains: ESO: Vault → k8s Secret automatic sync

hints: refreshInterval: auto-sync when Vault secret rotates
ExternalSecret → creates real k8s Secret: app uses standard secretKeyRef
Vault RBAC: only payment-service role can read secret/payments/prod

exercises.title: What happens when ConfigMap is updated?
exercises.instructions:
ConfigMap with application.yml is updated (changed log level from INFO to DEBUG).
1. If mounted as volume: when does the file update?
2. Does Spring Boot auto-reload the new value?
3. If injected as env var: when does the app see the new value?
4. What is the safest way to apply a config change?
exercises.expected_solution:
1. Volume-mounted ConfigMap: updates within ~1-2 minutes (k8s syncs ConfigMaps periodically).
   The file at /app/config/application.yml will show the new content.
   But: the JVM and Spring context have already loaded the old value.

2. Spring Boot does NOT auto-reload from file updates by default.
   Even though the file changed, the already-injected @Value beans hold the old value.
   Exception: Spring Cloud Config with @RefreshScope + actuator /actuator/refresh endpoint.
   With spring-cloud-starter-config + @RefreshScope: POST /actuator/refresh → reloads.
   Most production services: restart the pods to pick up new config.

3. Env var from ConfigMap/Secret: NEVER updates while the pod is running.
   Env vars are set at pod startup. Changing the ConfigMap does not change running pod env vars.
   To pick up new env var values: pod must be restarted.

4. Safest way to apply config change:
   Option A: Update ConfigMap → kubectl rollout restart deployment/payment-service
             → rolling restart picks up new file
   Option B: Spring Cloud Config with @RefreshScope → POST /actuator/refresh
             → hot reload without restart (but complex setup)
   Option C: Immutable ConfigMaps → create new ConfigMap version → update deployment to reference new version
             → rolling update ensures all pods use new config simultaneously
   Recommendation: Option A (rolling restart) for simplicity.

test_cases.expected:
1. Volume-mounted ConfigMap: updates within ~1-2 minutes (k8s syncs ConfigMaps periodically).
   The file at /app/config/application.yml will show the new content.
   But: the JVM and Spring context have already loaded the old value.

2. Spring Boot does NOT auto-reload from file updates by default.
   Even though the file changed, the already-injected @Value beans hold the old value.
   Exception: Spring Cloud Config with @RefreshScope + actuator /actuator/refresh endpoint.
   With spring-cloud-starter-config + @RefreshScope: POST /actuator/refresh → reloads.
   Most production services: restart the pods to pick up new config.

3. Env var from ConfigMap/Secret: NEVER updates while the pod is running.
   Env vars are set at pod startup. Changing the ConfigMap does not change running pod env vars.
   To pick up new env var values: pod must be restarted.

4. Safest way to apply config change:
   Option A: Update ConfigMap → kubectl rollout restart deployment/payment-service
             → rolling restart picks up new file
   Option B: Spring Cloud Config with @RefreshScope → POST /actuator/refresh
             → hot reload without restart (but complex setup)
   Option C: Immutable ConfigMaps → create new ConfigMap version → update deployment to reference new version
             → rolling update ensures all pods use new config simultaneously
   Recommendation: Option A (rolling restart) for simplicity.

hints: Volume mount: file updates automatically, but Spring doesn't reload
Env var: set at startup, never changes while pod runs
kubectl rollout restart: triggers rolling restart with new config

exercises.title: Full Razorpay k8s config setup
exercises.instructions:
Build complete Razorpay config management:
1. ConfigMap: application.yml with prod settings
2. Secret: db_password, redis_password, visa_api_key
3. Deployment: mounts config + injects secrets
4. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): app reads properties correctly
hints: @TestPropertySource: simulates what ConfigMap/Secret env vars would set
Default values: ${prop:default} for test safety
Service tests: verify injection works; k8s YAML is infrastructure

exercises.title: Startup probe for slow-starting services
exercises.instructions:
Some Spring Boot services take 90+ seconds to start (Flyway migrations, ML model loading).
1. Configure startupProbe to allow long startup
2. After startup complete: readiness + liveness probes take over
3. @SpringBootTest: verify health endpoints available
4. Explain: why startupProbe is better than long initialDelaySeconds
hints: startupProbe: runs first; once it passes, readiness+liveness start
failureThreshold × periodSeconds = maximum startup time allowed
initialDelaySeconds=0 on liveness/readiness when startupProbe is configured

exercises.title: RBAC for service account secrets access
exercises.instructions:
Create RBAC for Razorpay payment service:
1. ServiceAccount: payment-service-sa
2. Role: can read Secrets in payments namespace
3. RoleBinding: bind SA to Role
4. Deployment: use ServiceAccount
5. Explain: why pod-level ServiceAccount access matters
exercises.expected_solution:
# k8s/rbac.yml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: payment-service-sa
  namespace: payments
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: payment-secret-reader
  namespace: payments
rules:
- apiGroups: [''] # core API group
  resources: ['secrets']
  resourceNames: ['payment-secrets']  # ONLY this specific Secret
  verbs: ['get']  # read only
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: payment-secret-binding
  namespace: payments
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: payment-service-sa
  namespace: payments
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: payment-secret-reader
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
---
# In Deployment spec:
# spec:
#   serviceAccountName: payment-service-sa
#   automountServiceAccountToken: false  # opt-in only

# WHY RBAC MATTERS:
# By default: every pod gets the 'default' ServiceAccount
# Default SA: often has more permissions than needed (especially in older clusters)
# Attack scenario: compromised payment pod → uses SA token → reads ALL secrets in namespace
# With specific SA + Role: compromised pod can ONLY read 'payment-secrets'
# resourceNames: ['payment-secrets'] → granular: not all secrets, just this one
# Principle of least privilege: pod gets exactly what it needs, nothing more
hints: resourceNames: limits to specific secret (not all secrets in namespace)
automountServiceAccountToken: false: disable automatic SA token mount if not needed
Principle of least privilege: grant minimum required access

exercises.title: Complete config pipeline: create → apply → verify
exercises.instructions:
Write a shell script that:
1. Creates ConfigMap from file
2. Creates Secret from environment variables
3. Applies deployment
4. Waits for rollout
5. Verifies health endpoint
6. Rolls back if health check fails
exercises.starter_code:
#!/bin/bash
# deploy.sh — Razorpay payment service deployment script
exercises.expected_solution:
#!/bin/bash
# deploy.sh — Razorpay payment service deployment script
set -e  # exit on any error

ENV=${1:-staging}
NAMESPACE='payments'

echo '=== Deploying payment-service to '$ENV' ==='

# 1. Create/update ConfigMap from environment-specific file:
kubectl create configmap payment-config \
  --from-file=application.yml=k8s/config/$ENV/application.yml \
  --namespace $NAMESPACE \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
# --dry-run=client -o yaml | apply: create OR update (idempotent)

# 2. Create/update Secret from environment variables:
kubectl create secret generic payment-secrets \
  --from-literal=db_password=$DB_PASSWORD \
  --from-literal=visa_api_key=$VISA_API_KEY \
  --namespace $NAMESPACE \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

# 3. Apply deployment:
kubectl apply -f k8s/overlays/$ENV/ --namespace $NAMESPACE

# 4. Wait for rollout (max 5 minutes):
if ! kubectl rollout status deployment/payment-service \
     --namespace $NAMESPACE --timeout=5m; then
  echo '=== ROLLOUT FAILED — rolling back ==='
  kubectl rollout undo deployment/payment-service --namespace $NAMESPACE
  exit 1
fi

# 5. Verify health:
POD=$(kubectl get pods -n $NAMESPACE -l app=payment-service \
     -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')
HEALTH=$(kubectl exec $POD -n $NAMESPACE -- \
         wget -qO- http://localhost:8080/actuator/health | python3 -c \
         'import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)["status"])')

if [ "$HEALTH" != 'UP' ]; then
  echo '=== HEALTH CHECK FAILED — rolling back ==='
  kubectl rollout undo deployment/payment-service --namespace $NAMESPACE
  exit 1
fi

echo '=== Deployment successful! Health: '$HEALTH' ==='
hints: --dry-run=client -o yaml | apply: idempotent create-or-update pattern
rollout status --timeout: CI-friendly — fails if rollout doesn't complete in time
kubectl exec: run command inside a running pod for verification

exercises.title: Complete M21 T3 synthesis — config + secrets + graceful shutdown + tests
exercises.instructions:
Build complete Razorpay config management synthesis:
1. @Value injection reading from test properties (simulating ConfigMap)
2. Server graceful shutdown configured
3. Health probes enabled and verified
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): config values, readiness, liveness

All 3 tests pass.
hints: @TestPropertySource: simulates what ConfigMap/Secret env vars inject
Sampling rate as double: @Value reads '0.1' string and converts to double
management.health.probes.enabled=true: required for /readiness /liveness endpoints
